title: Cat Over-grooming Lower Belly: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat licking their belly bald? Learn about psychogenic alopecia, allergy triggers, and when to see a vet in our empathetic triage guide.
content_html:
What is Lower Belly Over-Grooming?

When a cat licks or chews their lower abdomen until the skin is bald, it is often referred to as psychogenic alopecia or over-grooming. This behavior is usually a response to either physical discomfort, such as itchy skin from allergies or parasites, or emotional distress, such as anxiety and boredom. Cats often use grooming as a self-soothing mechanism, which can unfortunately lead to significant fur loss and skin irritation.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for over-grooming is Low. While it is concerning to see your pet lose hair, it is typically a chronic issue rather than an acute medical emergency. You should schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to rule out underlying causes. However, if you notice the skin is bleeding, oozing, or smells foul, this may indicate a secondary infection that requires more prompt attention.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a clear, high-resolution photo of the bald area can be incredibly helpful for a remote or initial assessment. A photo allows the vet to see the pattern of hair loss and the condition of the skin without the cat hiding or grooming during the exam. It also serves as a baseline to track whether the patch is growing or if new hair is beginning to sprout after treatment begins.

faqs.question: Could my cat have a flea allergy even if I don't see fleas?
faqs.answer: Yes, many cats are hypersensitive to flea saliva. Even a single bite from a flea can trigger an intense grooming reaction that leads to baldness.

faqs.question: Can household changes cause over-grooming?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Cats are sensitive to their environment. A new pet, a recent move, or even changes in your work schedule can cause stress-related over-grooming.

faqs.question: Will the hair ever grow back?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Once the underlying cause—whether it is medical or behavioral—is identified and managed, the hair follicles usually recover and fur regrows.
